text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
ONCOLOGY RESTAGING — THIRD INTERVAL STUDY

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old male with known Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, primary site skin of the upper back. Patient has been on dabra 150 mg twice daily and tram 2 mg once daily since initial diagnosis. Prior restaging studies demonstrated excellent partial response at three months and disease stabilization at six months. Current study obtained for third interval restaging at approximately eight months of targeted therapy to evaluate for continued disease control versus progression.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following uneventful administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast via right antecubital vein, with images acquired in the portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations generated. Comparison made to prior CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast obtained approximately two months prior (six-month restaging study), and to the original baseline staging CT.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear. No new pulmonary nodules identified. No consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. The airways are patent to the segmental level. No endobronchial lesions. Previously noted absence of pulmonary nodular disease is maintained.

Mediastinum and hila: The heart is normal in size. The pericardium is unremarkable without effusion or thickening. Mediastinal contours are within normal limits. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy by size criteria. The great vessels are unremarkable. No anterior or posterior mediastinal masses.

Chest wall and pleura: No pleural thickening or effusion. No chest wall or rib lesions. The sternum is intact.

Axillae: The right axillary region demonstrates a markedly enlarged and heterogeneous conglomerate nodal mass, now measuring approximately 3.4 x 2.8 cm in the axial plane. This represents a substantial and worrisome increase compared to the prior six-month restaging study, at which time this mass measured 1.7 cm in its longest dimension. At the nadir following initiation of targeted therapy (three-month scan), this mass had decreased to 1.9 cm from its pre-treatment baseline of 4.8 x 3.9 cm. The current enlargement therefore represents a reversal of the prior treatment response. The mass demonstrates heterogeneous attenuation with areas of irregular peripheral enhancement and internal low-attenuation zones, morphologically similar to the appearance at baseline, suggesting a combination of viable tumor and central necrotic or cystic change. The overlying subcutaneous soft tissues show no definite skin involvement, though the deep subcutaneous planes are partially obscured. There is no discrete right axillary lymph node separate from this dominant mass, but several small adjacent lymph nodes measuring up to 0.8 cm are noted, increased in number compared to prior.

The left axilla is without enlarged lymph nodes. Left cervical region: previously enlarged left cervical level II and III lymph nodes, which had markedly decreased on prior studies and were no longer clearly enlarged, again appear borderline to mildly enlarged. The largest left cervical node now measures approximately 1.1 cm in the short axis — previously this was below threshold on the six-month scan. This finding is nonspecific but in the context of progressive disease elsewhere, is concerning for early nodal re-emergence.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is mildly enlarged in volume compared to the prior study, an interval change from the near-normal hepatic volume seen at the nadir. Multiple hepatic lesions are identified; the pattern is markedly changed and progressed from the most recent prior examination:

— Segment VII: A hypodense lesion previously measuring 1.0 cm on the six-month scan has grown substantially and now measures approximately 2.8 x 2.4 cm. On the three-month scan this lesion had measured 1.1 cm, and at original staging it measured 3.1 x 2.7 cm. The current lesion nearly recapitulates its initial size and demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement with central low attenuation consistent with central necrosis. The lesion abuts the right hepatic vein without definitive vascular invasion, but the interface is narrow and warrants close attention on follow-up.

— Segment II: A hypodense lesion previously measuring 0.5 cm (nadir) has enlarged to approximately 1.6 x 1.4 cm. At original staging this measured 1.8 x 1.5 cm. The current lesion demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement pattern consistent with viable metastatic melanoma.

— Segment IV: A new hypodense lesion measuring 1.3 x 1.1 cm is identified in the medial aspect of segment IV. This was not present on any prior examination. It demonstrates the same peripheral enhancement and central hypoattenuation pattern as the other hepatic lesions. This is a new hepatic metastasis.

— Segment VI: An additional new hypodense lesion measuring approximately 0.9 x 0.8 cm is identified in the posteroinferior right lobe (segment VI). This was not present on any prior imaging. Enhancement pattern is similar to other hepatic metastases. This represents a second new hepatic lesion.

In total, four discrete hepatic lesions are identified on the current study, compared to two lesions (the segment VII and segment II lesions) that remained measurable on the most recent prior scan. The aggregate hepatic tumor burden has increased substantially. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The portal and hepatic veins remain patent on this contrast-enhanced study. No perihepatic ascites.

Biliary system: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No gallstones. No biliary ductal dilatation. Common bile duct measures 4 mm.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation, measuring approximately 10.8 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesions. No splenic hilar adenopathy.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in morphology and attenuation throughout head, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic soft tissue stranding.

Adrenal glands: Both adrenal glands are unremarkable in size and morphology. No adrenal nodules or masses. No new adrenal lesions compared to prior.

Kidneys and ureters: The kidneys are normal in size, shape, and enhancement. No renal masses or hydronephrosis bilaterally. No perirenal soft tissue lesions. No ureteral abnormality.

Gastrointestinal tract: The visualized bowel loops throughout the abdomen demonstrate no focal wall thickening, obstruction, or abnormal enhancement. No evidence of bowel wall abnormality that would suggest direct involvement by metastatic disease. No free air or free intraperitoneal fluid.

PERITONEUM AND RETROPERITONEUM:

Lymph nodes: A newly enlarged right external iliac lymph node is identified, measuring approximately 1.2 x 0.9 cm in the short axis. This lymph node was not discretely identifiable on the prior restaging studies and represents a new site of presumptive metastatic involvement. No other enlarged pelvic or retroperitoneal lymph nodes are identified by size criteria at this time, though several borderline-sized para-aortic lymph nodes in the low retroperitoneum (up to 0.9 cm short axis) are noted — these were not clearly present on prior studies and warrant follow-up.

No free intraperitoneal fluid or peritoneal nodularity to suggest overt peritoneal carcinomatosis.

PELVIS:

Bladder: The urinary bladder is well-distended and smooth-walled. No filling defects or wall thickening.

Prostate and seminal vesicles: The prostate is mildly enlarged in volume (estimated 35 mL), consistent with age-appropriate benign change. No focal prostatic lesions to suggest involvement by metastatic disease. The seminal vesicles are unremarkable.

Bony pelvis: The bony pelvis is intact. No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified within the pelvis.

SOFT TISSUES:

Subcutaneous soft tissues: Multiple new cutaneous and subcutaneous nodular lesions are identified on the current study, distributed across the posterior and lateral trunk and the abdomen. These were not present on the prior restaging study and represent new metastatic deposits:

— A nodule along the posterior right flank/back region measures approximately 1.7 x 1.4 cm with slight soft tissue density enhancement.
— Two smaller nodules along the right lateral abdominal wall measure approximately 1.1 cm and 0.9 cm respectively.
— At least two additional smaller sub-centimeter nodular foci along the lower back are noted (measuring approximately 0.6-0.8 cm), which are new compared to prior.

Previously noted subcutaneous nodules that had resolved by the time of the six-month restaging study are not re-identified. The current subcutaneous lesions appear to represent new sites of disease rather than recurrence of previously resolved deposits. The overall pattern of extensive de novo subcutaneous spread is in keeping with the biology of BRAF/MEK inhibitor resistance.

Bone windows: The visualized osseous structures including the thoracic and lumbar vertebral bodies, ribs, sternum, and bony pelvis demonstrate no lytic, sclerotic, or expansile lesions to suggest osseous metastatic involvement on soft tissue windows. Cortical integrity appears maintained throughout.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. CLEAR INTERVAL DISEASE PROGRESSION compared to the prior six-month restaging study, representing acquired resistance to dabra and tram after a period of excellent and durable initial response.

2. RIGHT AXILLARY CONGLOMERATE MASS: Marked re-growth, now measuring approximately 3.4 x 2.8 cm, having more than doubled in size since the prior study (previously 1.7 cm). This mass nearly recapitulates its pre-treatment dimensions.

3. HEPATIC METASTASES: Significant progression of known hepatic disease with enlargement of the segment VII and segment II lesions (the former nearly reconstituted to its original baseline size), and two entirely new hepatic lesions in segments IV and VI. A total of four hepatic metastases are now present. No biliary obstruction.

4. NEW RIGHT EXTERNAL ILIAC LYMPH NODE: A newly apparent 1.2 cm right external iliac lymph node represents a new site of nodal involvement not previously identified.

5. NEW SUBCUTANEOUS AND CUTANEOUS NODULES: Multiple new soft tissue nodules are scattered across the posterior trunk, flank, and lateral abdominal wall — the largest measuring 1.7 cm — consistent with new cutaneous/subcutaneous metastatic deposits. Previously resolved subcutaneous disease has re-emerged in new locations, reflecting systemic escape from targeted therapy.

6. LEFT CERVICAL LYMPH NODES: Previously resolved left cervical lymphadenopathy has re-emerged with a borderline-enlarged node (1.1 cm short axis), which is new/progressive in the context of overall systemic progression.

7. NO NEW PULMONARY NODULES. No pleural, pericardial, or adrenal involvement identified. No osseous lesions on soft tissue windows.

8. NO CNS IMAGING PERFORMED ON THIS STUDY — dedicated brain MRI should be considered in the setting of systemic progression for complete restaging.

The overall pattern is most consistent with progressive systemic metastatic melanoma reflecting acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibitor targeted therapy. Urgent oncology review is warranted. Clinical correlation with laboratory tumor markers (LDH) is recommended.
